coredata.dc:title: Polymeric Blends of Carboxymethyl Cellulose and Sodium Alginate Containing Functionalized Carbon Dots Result in Stable and Efficient Fluorescent Films for Silver and Iron (III) Sensing
coredata.dc:creator: da Silva Júnior, Afonso Henrique
Macuvele, Domingos Lusitâneo Pier
Riella, Humberto Gracher
Soares, Cíntia
Padoin, Natan
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescent film using a polymeric blend of carboxymethyl cellulose and alginate with functionalized carbon dots (CDs) incorporated to detect Ag+ and Fe3+ was reported for the first time. CDs were hydrothermally obtained from the cambuci fruit. The film and the carbon dots obtained were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer, x-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy, thermogravimetric analysis, differential scanning calorimetry, fluorescence spectroscopy, diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, mechanical tests, Zeta potential, wettability, and swelling. Also, ionic stability and fluorescence were evaluated. The carbon dots obtained had an average diameter of 4.12 nm and a quantum yield of 4.17%. The polymeric blend with CDs nanoparticles exhibited improved thermal stability and tensile mechanical properties when compared to a pristine blend. The films presented an improved detection performance for Ag+ and Fe3+, considering synthetic solutions and real water samples (drinking water and lake water). The dynamic detection range for silver and iron (III) ions was 50–500 μM and the detection limits were 29.18 μM and 23.66 μM, respectively. Therefore, the film presented acceptable performance for detecting Ag+ and Fe3+ in synthetic solutions and real samples. This is a promising alternative compared to conventional techniques due to the easy synthesis, satisfactory analytical performance, stability, eco-friendly, and low-cost.

head.ce:title: Polymeric Blends of Carboxymethyl Cellulose and Sodium Alginate Containing Functionalized Carbon Dots Result in Stable and Efficient Fluorescent Films for Silver and Iron (III) Sensing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescent film using a polymeric blend of carboxymethyl cellulose and alginate with functionalized carbon dots (CDs) incorporated to detect Ag+ and Fe3+ was reported for the first time. CDs were hydrothermally obtained from the cambuci fruit. The film and the carbon dots obtained were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer, x-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy, thermogravimetric analysis, differential scanning calorimetry, fluorescence spectroscopy, diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, mechanical tests, Zeta potential, wettability, and swelling. Also, ionic stability and fluorescence were evaluated. The carbon dots obtained had an average diameter of 4.12 nm and a quantum yield of 4.17%. The polymeric blend with CDs nanoparticles exhibited improved thermal stability and tensile mechanical properties when compared to a pristine blend. The films presented an improved detection performance for Ag+ and Fe3+, considering synthetic solutions and real water samples (drinking water and lake water). The dynamic detection range for silver and iron (III) ions was 50–500 μM and the detection limits were 29.18 μM and 23.66 μM, respectively. Therefore, the film presented acceptable performance for detecting Ag+ and Fe3+ in synthetic solutions and real samples. This is a promising alternative compared to conventional techniques due to the easy synthesis, satisfactory analytical performance, stability, eco-friendly, and low-cost.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are a hot topic in materials science due to their excellent and unexpected properties [1–3]. These materials present good water dispersibility, excellent fluorescent stability, biocompatibility, easy synthesis, low cytotoxicity, easy functionalization, and good biocompatibility [4,5]. For these reasons, CDs have promising applications in various areas such as metal ions detection, bioimaging, photocatalysis, drug delivery, and others [6,7].

In the last 100 years, environmental concerns became a central topic in practically all fields. Heavy metals such as silver and iron in high concentrations became a challenge to human health and aquatic microorganisms [8,9]. Spectroscopic, fluorescence, electroanalytic and other methods are commonly used to analyze these metals. More recently, the fluorescence methods that use carbon dots have attracted much attention in detecting heavy metals. However, it has some drawbacks, such as non-reusability and stability (agglomeration) of CDs [10].

For this reason, the combination of different biopolymers (blends) and the addition of CDs in the matrix has been a strategy to ensure better performance of the material, more suitable characteristics for the final application, greater mechanical resistance, among other features [11,12]. In this sense, polymeric films such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [13], acrylamide monomers and crosslinking agent N,N′-methylenebis-acrylamide [14], chitosan, carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and sodium alginate (SA) [15] have been applied as a matrix for CDs for metals sensing. Chitosan has been used as a matrix for CDs. However, it is expensive and can influence the film's fluorescent characteristics, generating doubts about the carbon dots’ real effectiveness in the material [16]. Thus, blending CMC and SA can be a promising alternative. The mixture of these two biopolymers will not raise doubts about the material's fluorescence after adding the CDs. Also, these polymers are cheaper than other biopolymers and provide improved properties to the film.

Therefore, the present study reports the production of hybrid films for the first time using a blend of carboxymethyl cellulose sodium salt and sodium alginate with incorporated CDs to detect Ag+ and Fe3+ ions. Firstly, the CDs functionalized with sodium hypophosphite were synthesized following a Box-Behnken experimental design. Then, the CDs were incorporated into the CMC-Na and SA's network to obtain the functionalized films' precursor polymer solution. Finally, the composites were tested on real samples (drinking water and lake water) to verify their applicability. Also, the structural, morphological, and fluorescent characteristics of the biofilms and the carbon dots were investigated.

Carboxymethyl cellulose sodium salt (Synth), sodium alginate (Sigma-Aldrich), glycerol (Synth), sodium hypophosphite monohydrate (Vetec), sodium chloride (Neon), sodium hydroxide (Synth), calcium nitrate tetrahydrate (Neon), silver nitrate (Cennabras), lead nitrate (Lafan), iron (III) nitrate nonahydrate (Synth), zinc (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Synth), aluminum nitrate nonahydrate (Vetec), gadolinium trinitrate hexahydrate (Aldrich Chemistry), tin (II) chloride (Vetec), lithium chloride (Synth), mercury (II) chloride (Vetec), manganese (II) chloride tetrahydrate (Vetec), barium chloride dihydrate (Sigma-Aldrich), magnesium chloride (Synth), potassium chloride (Nuclear), copper dinitrate trihydrate (Neon), nickel (II) nitrate hexahydrate (Êxodo Científica), iron (II) chloride (Êxodo Científica), and cambuci fruit juice (Campomanesia phaea).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs using Box-Behnken experimental design
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 15 g of crushed cambuci fruit paste and 15 g of sodium hypophosphite solution, in concentrations of 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 M, was placed in a hydrothermal reactor coated internally with teflon at 90, 120, and 150 °C for 2, 4, and 6 h [17,18]. After the synthesis reaction, the following steps were purification, centrifugation for 30 min and filtration with 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membranes. Finally, the material was stored at a temperature of 4 °C for further characterization and film production. To evaluate the effects of NaPO2H2 concentration, temperature, and time on the synthesis of CDs, a Box-Behnken experimental design was applied [19]. Table 1 shows all levels coded for the variables under analysis.

The production of the film followed the methodology of Yaqin You with modifications [20]. For the film preparation, the CDs with higher quantum yield from the Box-Behnken experimental design were used. A mixture of 50 mL of distilled water with 3.5 g of glycerol, 0.75 g of CMC-Na, and 0.25 g of SA was added in a beaker and stirred until complete dilution at 60 °C. Then, 3 g of CDs solution were added to the mixture. After homogenization, the gel formed for 10 min was centrifuged. Finally, the mixture was added with small movements to one side and the other on a glass plate and left 24 h at 40 °C. The films synthesized without and with CDs were denoted as CMC/SA and CMC/SA/CDs, respectively.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the carbon dots were obtained by the JEM-1011 TEM (100 kV) equipment. The Olympus IX83 inverted microscope captured the films' photographs in the bright field and in the blue field with a 17-megapixel color digital camera from the Olympus DP73. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image was obtained using the Nanosurf equipment of the EasyScan 2 FlexAFM model. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the materials (about 10 mg) were determined in a Netzsch equipment, model STA 449 F3, with a temperature program of 25–800 °C, a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 and flow rate of 60 mL min−1 in nitrogen atmosphere. Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometry (FTIR) identified the samples' functional groups using the model equipment Agilent Technologies – Cary 660 FTIR. KBr tablets were used to analyze CDs, while the ATR-FTIR mode was applied to evaluate the films. The Rigaku MiniFlex600 DRX equipment performed the x-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis under Cu–Kα (λ = 0.154 nm) radiation and 2θ angle range from 10˚ to 70˚. The interlayer distance (d) was calculated using Bragg’s law (Eq. (1)), based on the position of C(002) [21]:
(1)
d =
n λ
2 sin ( θ )
where n is a positive integer Eq. (1), λ is the wavelength of incident x-ray, and θ is the angle between the incident rays and the materials' surface.

The emission and excitation spectra were collected using the multi-reader Infinite M200 TECAN. The diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was performed on a Perkin Elmer model Lambda 45 equipped with Labsphere RSA-PE-20. The surface load of the material was studied in the equipment Malvern Zetasizer Nanosizer at 25 °C. Raman spectroscopy measurements were performed using Anton Paar Cora 5200 Raman equipped with laser source at 785 nm. The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were performed by the Jade-DSC (Perkin Elmer) equipment equipped with an Intracooler 2P system. About 10 mg of the film were used and the process was carried out in a nitrogen atmosphere with flow rate of 20 mL min−1 and heating from 25 to 400 °C at a rate of 10 °C min−1. The mechanical properties of the films were determined by the equipment model TA.HD.plus Texture Analyzer following the ASTM D882–02 standard in which the samples were cut into a rectangular shape (2.54 × 7.5 cm) [22]. The gripper's initial separation was fixed at 40 mm and the crosshead speed was fixed at 0.01 mm s−1. This test was repeated five times for each specimen to confirm its repeatability. The thickness of the films was determined using a Mitutoyo’s digital micrometer. The atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) was performed on the Varian AA140 equipment.

The quantum yield of the carbon dots' photoluminescence was determined by Eq. (2) by treating data from an absorbance line (0.01–0.1) versus the area under the emission spectrum. The standard used was quinine sulfate (54%) due to the similarity of the spectrum with the synthesized material [23]:

where QY is the quantum yield, m is the slope of the curves, and η is the solvents' refractive index. The subscripts S and ST refer to the samples and the standard, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of swelling and wettability of films
ce:section.ce:para:
The contact angles of the films were performed using the Ramé-Hart 250 equipment. Ten simultaneous readings were collected for each film sample. To evaluate the films' swelling behavior with and without carbon dots (thickness of approximately 0.33 mm), the materials were cut in equal sizes and dried for 24 h at 100 °C. Subsequently, the films were weighed and immersed in distilled water (about 10 mL) at room temperature for 24 h. Then, the films were removed and dried on paper towels to ensure complete removal of surface water from materials and finally weighed. The values of the swelling ratio were obtained according to Eq. (3) [22]:
(3)
S
%
=
W SWOLLEN
−
W DRIED
W DRIED
× 100 %
where S is the percentage of the films' swelling ratio, and W is the weight of dry and swollen films.

For the evaluation of the sensitivity and selectivity of the synthesized film, tests were carried out with different metallic solutions (Ca2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Al3+, Gd3+, Sn2+, Li+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Ba2+). The film was immersed for 60 s and agitated in aqueous samples containing the respective ions (1 mM). Subsequently, the excess was removed with paper towels from the composite and left to stand for 1 h before reading the fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The film showed sensitivity for the metals Ag+ and Fe3+. In addition to attenuating fluorescence, the film also changed color (from yellowish to black when in contact with the Ag+ solution and reddish to Fe3+). Then, tests were performed with different concentrations of the metal (0–500 μ M). The Stern-Volmer ratio was used to describe the fluorescence quenching mechanism of the film in the presence of different ion concentrations (Eq. (4)) [24,25]:

where σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescent intensity of ten blanks, and m is the standard curve slope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ag+ and Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To carry out the experiments with real samples, aliquots of drinking water and lake water (27°33′38″ S, 48°27′13″ W) were used and the standard addition method was utilized to evaluate the feasibility of the film in detecting the Ag+ and Fe3+ ions. Firstly, the aqueous samples were centrifuged for 30 min and then filtered with 0.22 µm cellulose acetate membranes to remove suspended particles. Thus, the film was immersed for 60 s and agitated in aqueous samples containing the respective ions. Subsequently, the excess was removed with paper towels from the composite and left to stand for 1 h before reading the fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

In this study, the Box-Behnken experimental design was applied to optimize the conditions for the synthesis of carbon dots, varying the concentration of sodium hypophosphite, temperature, and reaction time. Table 2 shows the fifteen runs carried out randomly and the respective responses (QY%). Clearly, low quantum yields were noticed for some tests and in other runs a QY above 2% was obtained. Assay 12, with concentration of 1.0 M NaPO2H2, temperature of 150 °C and reaction time of 6 h, presented the highest quantum yield (4.17%). A similar QY was observed in CDs from dextrose modified by disodium phosphate [28]. The difference in quantum yield can be related to methodological differences in the manufacture of CDs, such as in the functionalizing salt, temperature of synthesis, and reaction time.

The experimental design proposed a polynomial model that statistically describes the CDs' production process (Eq. (6)). The model represented by Eq. (6) has the dimensionless variables x
1 , x
2 , and x
3 corresponding to the concentration of sodium hypophosphite, temperature, and reaction time, respectively.

Eq. (6) shows that the QY response is greatly influenced by time and temperature. Analyzing the coded coefficients, the x
2

2 , x

2
2 show great significance in the QY's final value with coded coefficients of 0.609, 0.759 and 0.537, respectively. These results agree with other studies that have evaluated the synthesis applying a Box-Behnken experimental design [19,29]. Thus, it is assumed that the Box-Behnken design for the synthesis of CDs used in this work is consistent with the literature. Factors x
2 and x
3 are mainly responsible for synthesizing nanoparticles, being associated to the carbonization and hydrolysis that occur during the CDs’ production [19].

The model’s evaluation was carried out through the Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), where each interaction and term’s values are shown in Table 3. The F value for the model was 8.98, with only a 1.3% chance of being due to noise. Table 3 shows that the p values were well above 0.05 for the terms x
1 , x
1
2 and x
1
x
2 , so we removed it from the proposed model. The values of the coefficients of determination (R2), the adjusted R2, and the predicted R2 of the model were 94.17%, 83.68%, and 7.31%, respectively. Another significant factor observed in ANOVA is the F value for Lack of Fit (95.17) concerning Pure Error.

Fig. S1(a) shows the predicted versus observed graph to verify the model’s reliability, presenting an R2 of 94.2%. This high value of the determination coefficient suggests that the proposed model is adequate to estimate the quantum yield value. Fig. S1(b) shows the graph of residuals by predicted QY values. The random dispersion and similar variance suggest an excellent experimental design for the CDs’ synthesis. Figs. S2–S4 demonstrate the effect of sodium hypophosphite concentration on the production of carbon dots. At concentrations of 0.5 M and 1.0 M, they show that the QY value was higher only at high temperature and high reaction time. Moreover, Figs. S5–S7 present the effect of temperature on the response under analysis. At 120 °C and 150 °C, there was a high QY in times above 5 h regardless of the sodium hypophosphite concentration. Also, Figs. S8–S10 show the effect of time on the synthesis of the nanoparticles. With 2 h of reaction time, a median QY value is observed at low temperature and low sodium hypophosphite concentration, suggesting a possibility of cost reduction depending on the application. With 6 h of reaction time, the highest quantum yield value is observed for the temperature range 135–150 °C.

The micrograph of the TEM analysis ( Fig. 1(a)) shows that the nanoparticles have almost spherical shape with diameter varying between 3 and 6 nm (Fig. 1(b)). The size of the CDs are in the same range as those obtained by other studies that found average diameters of 4 and 7 nm for the material synthesized from Ocimum sanctum [30]. The XRD analysis illustrated in Fig. 1(c) showed an amorphous material with a diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 22.7˚ and a weaker peak at 2θ = 28.4˚. The sharpest peak has an interlayer distance of the CDs of d = 0.391 nm. A similar value for the interlayer distance of synthesized CDs from waste paper for hydrothermal printing (d = 0.393 nm) was reported [31]. Because d is greater than that of graphite (0.33 nm), CDs are not graphical and have a low crystalline nature.

The synthesized CDs were also analyzed by FTIR. Fig. 2 shows the presence of characteristic bands of –OH/–NH (3426 cm−1), C–H (2917 cm−1), C–H (2859 cm−1), C˭O (1710 cm−1), C˭C (1643 cm−1), –NH2 (1454 cm−1), –OH (1363 cm−1), C–O (1176 cm−1) and CH2 (898 cm−1) [26,32,33]. CDs obtained from tomatoes presented similar bands [34]. Also, peaks of 1047 cm−1 and 819 cm−1 are attributed to the groups PO2 and PH2, respectively [35,36]. Therefore, these bands confirm P–H and P–O groups in the CDs structure. Additionally, there are many electronegative groups in the FTIR analysis, which corroborates with the Zeta potential of − 3.61 ± 0.814 mV obtained for the material in solution.

To assess the ionic stability of the carbon dots solution, fluorescence spectra were obtained at different concentrations of NaCl (0–1.0 M). Fig. S11(a) shows that the material has excellent ionic stability in the presence of sodium chloride, even in high concentration, with a very slight decrease in fluorescence. Moreover, the dependence of the CDs’ fluorescence with the variation of the pH (3, 5, 7, and 9) was evaluated. Fig. S11(b) shows that the increase of the pH causes the reduction of the fluorescence.

Fig. 3(a) shows the DRS analysis for CMC/SA, CMC/SA/CDs, CMC/SA/CDs/Ag+, and CMC/SA/CDs/Fe3+. In the range from 400 nm to 900 nm, the films showed low reflectivity compared to CMC/SA/CDs after immersion in the respective solutions with the metallics ions [37]. This result suggests that the ions are impregnated on the films surfaces (metal oxides). To understand the process, an AAS was performed separately with silver and iron (III) ions before and after the immersion of the CMC/SA/CDs. The AAS analysis showed that the silver concentration decreased from 98.48 to 97.70 μM and the iron (III) concentration also decreased from 96.22 to 95.40 μM. The porous morphology of the films with and without carbon dots are visualized by the inset photographs in Fig. 3(a). The photographs suggest that the carbon dots possibly interacted with the polymeric matrix by two mechanisms: (i) the degree of crosslinking increased due to the binders present in the nanoparticles; and (ii) the CDs functionalized the surface of the films, optimizing the fluorescence properties of the material to act as a sensor (Fig. S12) [38].

Fig. 3(b) shows the emission spectrum of the CMC/SA/CDs film. The fluorescence spectrum was excited by wavelengths in the range of 260–440 nm. When the excitation wavelength varied from 260 nm to 360 nm, a gradual increase in the material’s luminescent intensity was observed with a maximum peak at 443 nm. Similar results for hybrid films with CDs in a PVA matrix were reported, with the maximum emission peak at 459 nm [39]. Fig. 3(c) shows the CMC/SA/CDs fluorescent stability when exposed to UV light for 240 min. The luminescent intensity varied slightly over that time. This suggests that the film presents fluorescent stability, which is an important characteristic for sensing applications.

The AFM analysis was performed to investigate the topology of the CMC/SA and CMC/SA/CDs films (Fig. S13(a-b)). The roughness mean square (RMS) of the material presents a value of 33.30 nm and 46.19 nm for the film without and with CDs, respectively. The roughness can significantly influence the optical, mechanical, and adsorptive properties of the film. Other work reported RMS values for pure CMC film and CMC film embedded with CDs of 1.24 nm and 3.88 nm, respectively [20]. These RMS differences reported in the literature and in the present study can be related to the polymer blend and the reticulation degree [22].

When assessing the degree of swelling (S%) of the films without and with CDs, one can observe that after 24 h in distilled water the film without the nanoparticles obtained 2020.12 ± 176%, while the film with CDs resulted in 138.29 ± 7%. These results show that the reduction in the degree of swelling when the CDs are inserted in the polymeric matrix can be related to the functional groups present in the nanoparticles responsible for the hydrophilicity and the crosslinking density [40,41].

The contact angles of the film without and with CDs were 28.74 ± 0.1˚ and 40.37 ± 0.1˚, respectively. The increase in the value of the contact angle with the nanoparticles’ presence can be explained by the increase in the hydrophobicity of the material due to the aromatic compounds of the CDs [42].

Fig. 3(d) shows the XRD analyzes for the CMC and SA polymers, and the CMC/SA/CDs film. In the XRD profile for the CMC powder, a crystalline peak is observed around 2θ = 20.33˚ and a sharp peak is noticed at 2θ = 35.26˚. For the sodium alginate powder, three peaks can be observed: the first at 2θ = 13.91˚, the second at 2θ = 22.13˚, and the third at 2θ = 37.84˚. The XRD profile for the CMC/SA/CDs film shows that the crystallinity of both polymers is reduced. Moreover, the peaks are displaced, showing a sharp peak at 2θ = 21.03˚ and a smaller peak at 2θ = 35.89˚. These differences are caused by the formation of new chemical bonds between polymers and the nanoparticles because of crosslinking and strong electrostatic interaction [43,44]. Similar values were reported previously [45,46].

The FTIR spectra of the films without and with CDs are shown in Fig. 2. Most of the observed peaks are shared between the three samples, indicating that the film with CDs retained the main functional groups. The widening of the peak by 3300 cm−1 in the spectrum indicates hydrogen bonds between the –OH groups of the film’s components (CMC, sodium alginate, glycerol, and carbon dots) [47]. The displacement of the peak 1654 cm−1 in CMC/SA to 1648 cm−1 in CMC/SA/CDs is due to the increase in the characteristic elongation of C˭C. The peaks at 2946 cm−1 and 2883 cm−1 in the CMC/SA film and at 2952 cm−1 and 2894 cm−1 in the CMC/SA/CDs are characteristic of C–H [48]. The peaks between 1654 cm−1 and 1419 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of COO- asymmetrical and symmetrical elongation of the polymers (CMC and SA) [49]. The peak at 1112 cm−1 in the film without CDs, moved to 1116 cm−1 in the material with CDs, is assigned to the group C–O–C [50]. Moreover, the peak at 1037 cm−1 for CMC/SA, shifted to 1029 cm−1 in CMC/SA/CDs, is due to the increase in the characteristic elongation of C–O present in the polymeric matrix after adding the CDs [51]. Therefore, based on the FTIR spectrum, the CDs were successfully impregnated in the polymer matrix.

Fig. 4(a) shows the Raman spectrum for the two synthesized films (CMC/SA and CMC/SA/CDs). The spectra show three common peaks (1239, 1578 and 1640 cm−1). However, another band present in CMC/SA and CMC/SA/CDs displaced and presented greater intensity in the film with the nanoparticles at 1367 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1, respectively. The band at 1239 cm−1 is characteristic of the following vibrational modes: COC stretching, v(COC), δ (HCC), δ (HCO), and δ (COH) [52]. The band at 1640 cm−1 corresponds to the vibrational stretching mode C˭C [53]. Therefore, due to the few changes in the CMC/SA and CMC/SA/CDs, the spectra suggests that the polymer matrix is preserved after insertion of CDs [52,54].

Fig. 4(b-c) shows the TGA analysis of films without and with the presence of CDs. The TG and DTG curves of CMC/SA thermal events are seen in 2 stages. The first event (35–150 °C) is attributed to the reduction of water content in the sample, resulting in a mass loss of approximately 18%. The second event that happens in the range of 150–300 °C is due to the thermal decomposition of CDs and CMC/SA, reaching a reduction of about 83% of the mass [55]. As for the CMC/SA/CDs, the TG and DTG curves thermal events are observed in 2 stages. The first event (40–180 °C) is attributed to the reduction of water content in the sample, resulting in a mass loss of approximately 23%. The second event that occurs in the range of 180–300 °C is due to the CDs and CMC/SA’s thermal decomposition, reaching a reduction of about 80% of the mass. Therefore, there is a slight improvement in the performance of thermal stability with the addition of CDs to the film, which can possibly be attributed to the interactions between the nanoparticles and the CMC/SA [56].

Fig. 4(d) shows the DSC analysis for both films produced (CMC/SA and CMC/SA/CDs). For the CMC/SA, an endothermic peak (136 °C) was observed in the temperature range of 25–185 °C referring to the volatile loss process, mainly water [57]. Likewise, two exothermic peaks are visualized at 265 °C and 274 °C, related to the degradation of the polymers [58]. The CMC/SA/CDs obtained an endothermic peak in the range of 25–185 °C (131 °C), related to the reduction of water content in the material. Also, exothermic peaks are visualized at 275 °C, 281 °C, and 335 °C, which refer to the decomposition/carbonization or melting of the material [59]. The differences between the two materials are related to chemical changes in the polymeric matrix, such as the higher crosslink density [14].

The investigation of the mechanical properties of a film is essential to evaluate its application. Fig. S14 shows the tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break for the films with and without carbon dots. The tensile strength increased with the addition of carbon dots to the polymer matrix, from 29.30 ± 2.25 MPa (CMC/SA) to 52.38 ± 1.56 MPa (CMC/SA/CDs). There was an increase in the TS of approximately 79%. CMC/SA/CDs’ TS enhancement suggests an increase of crosslinking reactions and interactions (ionic and hydrogen bonds) between CMC, SA, and CDs molecules [49].

In contrast, the elongation at break was lower for CMC/SA/CDs than CMC/SA, 23.54 ± 3.65% and 26.41 ± 2.17%, respectively. This result may be related to the increased crosslinking density and electrostatic forces between the substances, which provide less flexibility to the film with carbon dots [60]. Similar values of tensile strength and elongation at break for pure CMC films and CMC/Alginate blend were reported in other studies, 31 MPa/~ 25.5% and 18.8 MPa /18.68%, respectively [20,61]. Moreover, another study with CMC/CDs films showed tensile strength and elongation values at break of 48 MPa and approximately 28.5%, respectively [20]. Therefore, the addition of CDs to the polymer blend improved the mechanical properties of the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of films in detecting Ag+ and Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: For the evaluation of the films in detecting metal ions, selectivity tests were first performed under the optimum conditions of the material (Ca2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Al3+, Gd3+, Sn2+, Li+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Ba2+). Fig. 5 shows that only for the metals Ag+ and Fe3+ there was an attenuation of the film’s fluorescence at a wavelength of 360 nm, showing a potential application as sensor for these two ions. Subsequently, solutions with different concentrations of metals (0–500 μ M) were separately prepared and their fluorescence spectra were evaluated. Fig. 6(a-b) illustrates the decrease in the photoluminescence of the films in the presence of silver and iron (III) when the concentration increased. Both cases presented excellent R2 values for the linear relationship between concentration and relative fluorescent intensity (Fig. 6(c-d)) in the range 0–500 μ M. The detection limit for silver and iron (III) ions was 29.18 and 23.66 μ M, respectively. Table 4 shows that, compared with other studies, the material obtained in this research showed similar analytical parameters. Fig. S15(a-d) qualitatively visualizes the films in the bright field through microscopy analysis. The films immersed in Ag+ and Fe3+ presented different colors (fundamental to differentiate them when analyzed). Fig. S16(a-d) shows the films in the blue filter. The sensitivity for these two metal ions can be explained by several factors, such as the structure of the polymer matrix/CDs, the various interactions between metals and CMC/SA/CDs, thermodynamic affinity, and the efficiency of chelate formation. In addition, the variability on the capacity of metals to form complexes, due to the existence of structural particularities preventing them to interact efficiently with the CMC/SA/CDs, could explain the results obtained in this work [62].

Tests on real samples were carried out with water aliquots for drinking and lake water. The samples collected for this experiment were analyzed via AAS to validate the results. Thus, 100, 200, and 300 μM of each metal were added separately. Table 5 shows that the amount measured was close to that added, with recovery values in the range of 95.1–98.6% for Ag+ and 93.9–99.3% for Fe3+. Also, a low RSD% ranged from 0.84 to 2.15 in water with Ag+ ions and from 1.02 to 5.45 with Fe3+. The results suggest that films are promising in detecting both metals, showing similar recovery values and RSD% with other studies in the literature [63,64]. Therefore, the excellent performance of the synthesized materials can be attributed to the presence of polymers (CMC-Na and SA), which does not allow the agglomeration of carbon dots in the sensitivity of ions [64].

A novel fluorescent film using a polymeric blend of carboxymethyl cellulose and sodium alginate containing carbon dots was synthesized. The carbon dots obtained were successfully modified using sodium hypophosphite and had an average size of 4.12 nm. The optimal synthesis condition resulted in a quantum yield of 4.17%. A statistical model was proposed, with R2 of 94.17% through the Box-Behnken’s experimental design, to optimize the CDs synthesis process. The nanoparticles exhibited excellent ionic stability and an interlayer distance of 0.39 nm. The film produced showed sensitivity to silver and iron (III) through the fluorescence quenching mechanism, RMS of 46.19 nm, tensile strength of 52.38 MPa, elongation at break of 23.54%, contact angle of 40.36˚, and good thermal stability. Additionally, it showed a wide dynamic range (50–500 μ M) and LOD of 29.18 μ M for Ag+ and 23.66 μ M for Fe3+. Thus, it is an attractive material when compared to conventional techniques for detecting these metals. The material proposed here is easy to synthesize, has satisfactory analytical performance, is low-cost, and eco-friendly.
